post.content:
Hey everyone ; I am just a fresher admitted in Computer science branch in a Tier-3 college this year (2021). No company comes to my college except some service base "WITCH" companies.

Will you please guide me how to crack internships and placements at good product based companies if I start coding from scratch now.

I am a hardworking person and can work for 8-10 hours .....

Looking for your comments ............

* Since u are still in uni , so focus on your gpa .

*  Pick any one language , say java , and start learning its basics - like - variables , for loop , while loop , function , recursion etc .. After understanding the theory ,  solve 40-50 easy **implementation** based questions from hackerrank . Also read some blogs on writting clean and well commented code .

* Once u have done that , then start learning basics of Data structures and algorithm either through blogs/YT channel or by any other means which u feel comfortable . After doing  that implement those algorithms by yourself and also implement basic functionalities of all the data structures  without using any library function .

* Once done with the basics , then start solving  topic wise **easy** questions from leetcode . Solve 15 - 20 questions from each topic and move ahead to another topic . Once you have enough confidence then move to medium difficulty problems . Once you have solved enough problems , say 100 - 150 , then start giving contests and make it a habit to go through all the solved/unsolved problems after the contest .

* Also try to see and note down the pattern in each question . After solving/understanding enough problems u will be able to see repeated patterns .

* Make it a habit to read previously solved problems every few weeks , else u will forget the concepts used in those question .

* Always write your code first on paper and manually test it with some test cases  . Make sure to always handle the edge test case manually on paper. And only after doing that write code on the editor .

* And also try to develop a habit of reading and understanding other's code as it will be very useful ahead in your career .

* Initially it may take time to solve questions but keep patience and don't loose hope . Just continue working hard with calmness . Since you are still in 1st year so give as much time as possible to a  particular problem . 

**GOOD LUCK !!**

Hi 
I made a big mistake by not studying programming language Basics  / DSA in my college days.
As you are in your college days. 
1)Focus on your GPA (in some companies if you are above 8 or 8.5 they will skip 1st round sometimes)
2)Spend daily at least 1hr for APTITUDE (which will be your 1st round in most of the companies)
3)Learn the programming language Basics( important) after that try to solve EASY prblms at least 40 -50 then go for Medium. 
4)Learn DSA which will help you to stand out.
5) Make projects, go for Intern.
Note: Consistency is the Key

I'll keep it short and crisp.
Be good at DSA, do CP (a lot if you love it, it is kind of secret sauce that everyone hesitates to disclose because they receive criticism for saying this), don't forget to build projects (start with web development if you want internships quickly, then learn React JS), be decent at communication (your ideas are only worthy if you're able to effectively convey them).

That's it, I'm graduating this year from "tier-3" skipped campus placement because I knew I could do better, and truly doing better. My secret sauce was I never considered myself from "tier-3", I trust myself and my potential, someone saying my college as "tier-3" doesn't mean anything to me, and that confidence always reflects in my personality that I got chances that most of my colleagues never got, because they never believed they could get because of entitling themselves to tier-3, don't ever do that, and you're good to crack any company with this.

One last thing, reach out to lot of people working at good companies and get your resume always examined by them, my resume has been examined by many just by making good connections, and hence it gets me shortlisted almost everytime. 

Good luck.

Everyone has given very valuable points here. 
Just one last piece of advice about the coding part - **start this practice from your 2nd year (4th semester) onwards** - along with coding, make a habit of talking through the logic first, and then write the code.
Either pair up with a friend, or do it yourself - as you wish.

First talk through the problem statement, explain the logic, talk about the data structures which you are going to use and why, and then write the code - and for that part as well - keep talking like here I am initializing the array, here I am traversing through the array and checking for this etc etc.

Target solving problems within 20 minutes alongwith this talking/explanation part.

**Reason :** In real interviews, the setup and expectation will be exactly the way mentioned above - the interviewers would expect you to cleanly explain your ideas alongwith writing code.

**Lastly**, if you feel that off-campus is not going to work out, then consider going for **MTech/PhD** from top institutes if interested. You can aim for GATE and get into IITs/NITs for MTech/MS/MTech+PhD (with handsome scholarship sponsored by Central Government of India - amount is sufficient to cover academic+hostel fees) if you get a great rank/percentile.

- Maintain a good GPA. - Learn programming (use the plan by aqu106 or any other tangible ones online). - Work on your communication skills as much as programming skills. The main problem you have in a Tier-3 college, as you mentioned, is good compnies don't come to you. So, it's simple, you have to go to them. There are several meritocratic ways you could score good internships and jobs. - Hackathons (especially ones with internship/job prizes) - Hiring Challenges hosted on coding websites - Things like Google summer of code or Google CodeJam. - Earn some awards in competitions which will help boost your profile. - Build a network in the industry, especially companies you'd like to work for. This goes a looong way. Referrals are the best shot at getting an interview. There are several startups that do amazing work, pay quite well and are generally more concerned about your competence than the college you go to. Try landing internships at such startups in your 2nd and 3rd year. Remember to be pragmatic. There's several amazing companies there doing good work and pay great. Don't restrict your definition of success to just a couple of big companies. All the best ^_^

Learn to code
Contribute opensource, learn technologies (web dev stacks/mobile dev) 
Give moderate time, develop cool projects and learn how development works

Pick C++/Java for
https://codeforces.com/contests
https://www.codechef.com/contests
Beware, being good here takes a lot of time (months to years), so don't get demotivated, target expert on cf and if you are enjoying it, you can reach cm.
Give this the most of your time (CP is about improving problem-solving and analytical skills and this helps literally everywhere not only in software development, don't listen to people saying otherwise)

Keep on the lookout for industry/research internships(whatever suits you) starting after the 1st year only, many companies like DE Shaw, Google have programmes then.

Once your intern/placement season starts, go here https://leetcode.com/

Apart from this, ofc keep your gpa 8+, don't waste too much time on movies/series, pick a regular physical exercise(running, gym, badminton, etc), socialize and make friends, it is tough to do so after college, and don't forget to enjoy the best years!!
Bro if you don\'t mind can i know your college name please
Keep grinding DSA and a bit of development. Make an habit of sending tons of connection requests in LinkedIn everyday which will bring to you a wide variety of opportunities.

Your Start Does not determine how you are going to finish.

Being from tier 3/ 4 college and currently holding an off campus offer from one of these companies(Wells Fargo, Bny Mellon,Deutsche Bank, Morgan Stanley and Amazon) I think I will be a perfect fit to answer this question.For now Practice dsa,Cover as many topics as possible try to participate  in leetcode contest.

Also join arsh goyal fam telegram group through which will get updated about different hiring event and hackathons.

Soon I will be posting my journey from my real account once my exams gets over.
I am also a victim and also I have here to post this kind of question......\nThanks\uD83D\uDC4C

As U are in College, Let me give u what I would have done If I were you Semester wise.
* First Semester pick any language Java, C++. Learn basics loop variables, recursion, or just skip learning basics part Just purchase a coding course DSA one. I will suggest Pepcoding their 
   Lev1, Lev2 could be done within first semester, or you can purchase Coding Ninjas like any course as It cause momentum and peer helps in coding journey.
* Second Semester Try competitive coding do it till the end of the degree as u have ur degree.
* Third Semester try Dev, make some project.
* Fourth Semester Try landing Winter Internship.
* Fifth semester, Sixth Semester try getting Internship. Continue doing competitive/ Dev what serves you, whatever u like.
* Voila in fourth year U will have best offer you could crack.
* Try maintaining your GPA.
Believe or not doing DSA and Development will give you confident to do better in GPA department.

As someone who was from a tier 3ish college let me tell you the real scenario this will be demotivating but this the truth, the chances of tier 3 fresher making it big as a fresher i.e. in 4th yr are extremely low, only 2-3 extremely lucky folks and top Competitive programmers make it big as a fresher. I'm not saying you cannot be one of those, but chances are low.

Until you get your first 1yr of experience you will be rejected in resume filtering itself, a good chunk of high paying companies straight up include IIT/NIT criteria in requirements. So don't get carried away from these SDE sheets etc. they are good for learning but don't expect big tech knocking at your door.

learn C++/python, DSA, computer fundamentals, keep cgpa above 8 preferably 8.5 throughout college, improve linkedin, apply for internships expect to not even get a response after applying, give hackathons and hope for the best, maybe prepare for GATE as well bcoz off-campus scenario mostly does not work out.
